items.title: State the correct interpretation
items.avoid: Writing '95% chance the true value is in the interval' in reports, PRs, or slide decks.
items.prefer: Use procedure language: '95% confidence interval' or 'interval from a method with 95% nominal coverage under repeated sampling.'
items.why: Misinterpretation erodes trust with statisticians and leads to wrong decisions when stakeholders treat the interval as a Bayesian posterior.
items.example: PR template: `Accuracy 85.0% (95% CI 81.2–88.1%, Wilson interval on n=1000 holdout).`

items.title: Match the interval method to the data type
items.avoid: Defaulting to Wald z intervals for proportions with small n or p̂ near 0 or 1.
items.prefer: Use Wilson, Clopper–Pearson, or Agresti–Coull for proportions; t intervals for means with unknown σ.
items.why: Wald intervals can have poor coverage and impossible bounds; better methods stay closer to nominal 95% coverage.
items.example: 8 successes in 50 trials: Wilson gives roughly [0.07, 0.27]; naive Wald can include negative lower bounds.

items.title: Respect the unit of independence
items.avoid: Bootstrapping rows when observations are grouped (users, sessions, prompts).
items.prefer: Resample clusters (user IDs, prompt IDs) so the bootstrap mimics the sampling design.
items.why: Ignoring clustering yields intervals that are too narrow and overstate certainty.
items.example: For 500 users with 5 ratings each, bootstrap 500 user-level aggregates, not 2500 rating rows.
